q3: The model consistently gave the 40 people in the retention program passive-voice treatment — 'a case manager to call,' 'emergency stabilization available' — even after I explained multiple times that the verbs needed to belong to the people, not the services. It understood the principle when applied to James but kept reverting to service-delivery framing when scaling to the group.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, and 'Participant Agency (priority #1)' is exactly right as the top priority. What it didn't fully capture is that agency has to persist grammatically — it's not just about narrative framing but about who owns the verbs sentence by sentence. The rubric helped the model get the big structure right but didn't prevent it from slipping on the sentence-level subject choices.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently strong on the donor framing criterion — 'you already bet on the part most funders overlook' came relatively early and held — but kept backsliding on participant agency specifically in the scaling section, which suggests the model treats the opening story and the impact numbers as separate zones with different rules rather than one continuous piece with consistent voice.

q3: The main recurring issue was a tendency to let the organization's voice or framing creep back in at the edges — phrases that subtly made the program the subject instead of the person. It wasn't egregious, but it needed nudging more than once. The 'standing between them' phrasing was a good example of catching something that sounded fine but implied the wrong power dynamic.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it captured my priorities more precisely than I would have articulated them upfront — especially the 'Participant Agency' framing and the 'Donor as Strategist' language. That said, I'm not sure how much of the good output came from the rubric versus the model just responding well to my in-conversation feedback. The rubric named things I care about, but the model was already moving in the right direction from my corrections.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model was actually quite consistent on the 'story-first, number-second' structure — it never led with statistics, which I appreciated and might not have consciously clocked without seeing it named. It also made me realize the 'Organization Decentered' criterion was the one the model needed the most help with in practice, even though it seemed to understand it in principle — there's a gap between knowing the rule and applying it in every sentence.
